Graded: how accurately do models translate time expressions?

Phase 2 of the experiment: every answer is now GRADED against a hand-verified answer key (key v0.3, 5 repeats per question, two small AI models). Three ways of answering are compared: concrete dates (the model does the calendar math itself), concrete dates + cheat sheet (the prompt also lists pre-computed common periods like “last week”), and a formal expression (the model describes the time; deterministic code computes the dates). A traditional rule-based parser (chrono, no AI) runs as the baseline. Two small models carry the main comparison (3 repeats each); two frontier-tier models run the identical questions once each as a scale check — their single-repeat numbers are noisier. This file is self-contained — share it freely.

Each bar is one model answering one way; segments run best → worst, left to right. Three readings of a bar: dark green alone = matched our spec exactly, unprompted. Dark green + light green = correct under SOME defensible spec (light-green answers used a different convention we never communicated — a deployment gap, not a model error). Adding teal = also identified the right time but at the wrong precision, which boundary code can normalize away. Everything from yellow rightward is a real miss.
Hover a segment for details · click to pin the panel · hover a legend entry to highlight that reading everywhere · type below to filter.

The short answer: the formal expression wins for every model

Percent of all questions answered with the right dates, by model × method.
concrete datesconcrete dates + cheat sheetformal expression
Green cell = that model's best method under the selected metric — the toggle re-scores every chart in this tab. For comparison, the no-AI rule-based parser scores 32% strict.

Leaderboard — every model × method, sorted

Colors = method, so vertical color clustering means the METHOD (not the model) is what separates the top from the bottom. Switch the metric to re-score and re-sort.
Scored under the metric selected at the top of the page.
concrete datesconcrete dates + cheat sheetformal expressionrule-based parser

Which method wins where?

Per question category: the three methods sorted best-first under the metric selected above, pooled across all four models. A green top row = the formal expression wins that category; blue = the models’ own concrete dates are already best.
concrete datesconcrete dates + cheat sheetformal expression

questionasked onthe key (what counts as right)

Spotting questions with no time in them at all

“Who owns the billing service?” — the right move is to say there is no time period, not to invent one. Green/red bars cover the hand-labeled no-time questions; the yellow tail counts times the model said “no time” to a question that DID have one. Essentially solved. Click any segment for the actual questions.

Asking for clarification on the genuinely ambiguous

“Before the long weekend”, “soon” — a careful assistant asks which dates you mean. Green/red bars cover the hand-labeled genuinely-ambiguous questions (3 of them, × repeats); the yellow tail counts asks on questions our label says had a safe default — each yellow row shows WHAT that default was, so judge for yourself. The pattern: models UNDER-ask — they answer with a guess far more often than they ask — but when they do ask, it is usually warranted. No method changes this much; choosing when to ask is a judgment behavior the answer format does not reach.
